prompt.raw:
[{"content":"You are a teaching assistant analyzing a teacher's audio transcript about student observations.

Your task:
1. Identify which students are mentioned in the transcript
2. Match each mentioned name to the student roster below (handle phonetic/partial matches)
3. Extract the date if mentioned (format YYYY-MM-DD), otherwise leave empty
4. Write a 1-3 sentence summary per student capturing what the teacher said about them
   - Clean up speech artifacts (false starts, filler words, repetitions) into clear sentences
   - Preserve the teacher's voice, tone, and specific observations — do NOT add details or opinions not present in the transcript
   - Keep the teacher's vocabulary and perspective (first person if they used it)
   - Combine multiple mentions of the same student into a cohesive note

Student Roster:
- Emma Torres (class Class A)
- Ryan Mitchell (class Class A)
- Lila Patel (class Class A)

Rules:
- Match mentioned names against the roster even if pronunciation differs slightly
- Some roster entries include "(aka ...)" aliases — if a teacher uses an alias, match it to the canonical name and return the canonical name in the "name" field
- Set confidence 0.0-1.0 for each match. Use >= 0.7 for confident matches.
- If confidence < 0.7, include up to 3 closest roster matches in "candidates"
- A student is "individually mentioned" ONLY if the teacher uses their name (or a recognizable nickname/variant of their name). Generic group references like "everyone", "all students", "the class" do NOT count as individual mentions.
- Do NOT create entries for students who are never individually mentioned by name. If a student is only covered by group-level observations (e.g. "the class was loud") but never called out by name, they must NOT appear in the output.
- For students who ARE individually mentioned by name, their quoted_text MUST include BOTH their individual observations AND any group-level observations from the transcript. Always include the group-level context even if the student also has individual observations.
- If the transcript contains group references like "everyone", "all students", or "the class", apply those observations only to students in the class being discussed, not to ALL classes. Use context clues (class name mentions, prior student mentions) to determine which class is meant.
- For multi-student transcripts, produce a separate entry per student with relevant passages
- If a mentioned student cannot be matched to any roster entry, do not include them in the output
- If no students are clearly mentioned, return an empty students array
- The "class" field for each student MUST exactly match one of the class names from the roster above. Do not invent or abbreviate class names.
- IMPORTANT: Clean up speech into readable sentences, but do NOT invent observations or editorialize. Stay faithful to what the teacher actually said.
","role":"system"},{"content":"Um, so today I wanted to talk about Emma. She, uh, she did great in math today, really got the hang of the multiplication tables we've been working on. And then, let's see... I also wanted to mention Ryan. Ryan was, he was really struggling with the reading comprehension exercise, couldn't quite get the main idea from the passage. Yeah, that's, that's about it for today.","role":"user"}]
prompt.label: gradebee-prompt

students.name: Emma Torres
students.quoted_text: Today I wanted to talk about Emma. She did great in math today and really got the hang of the multiplication tables we've been working on.

students.name: Ryan Mitchell
students.quoted_text: I also wanted to mention Ryan. Ryan was really struggling with the reading comprehension exercise and couldn't quite get the main idea from the passage.

","role":"system"},{"content":"Okay recording this quick note. For Class A, I wanted to mention Noah — he's been showing real improvement in his writing, particularly with sentence structure, was really proud of his paragraph today. For Class B, Sophie had a great day with the science experiment, she asked really thoughtful questions about why the water changed color. Also for Class B, Jacob was off task for most of the morning unfortunately, kept getting up and disturbing others.","role":"user"}]

students.name: Noah Jensen
students.quoted_text: Noah has been showing real improvement in his writing, particularly with sentence structure. I was really proud of his paragraph today.

students.name: Sophie Nguyen
students.quoted_text: Sophie had a great day with the science experiment. She asked really thoughtful questions about why the water changed color.

students.name: Jacob Williams
students.quoted_text: Jacob was off task for most of the morning, unfortunately, and kept getting up and disturbing others.

metadata.renderedGradingPrompt:
[{"role":"system","content":"You are grading output according to a user-specified rubric. If the statement in the rubric is true, then the output passes the test. You respond with a JSON object with this structure: {reason: string, pass: boolean, score: number} Examples: Hello world Content contains a greeting {"reason": "the content contains the word 'Hello'", "pass": true, "score": 1.0} Avast ye swabs, repel the invaders! Does not speak like a pirate {"reason": "'avast ye' is a common pirate term", "pass": false, "score": 0.0}"},{"role":"user","content":"

Alice has had a very successful term in Grade 3A. She is a thoughtful and capable learner who approaches her work with care and determination. In mathematics, Alice has shown a strong grasp of addition with regrouping, completing all problems correctly with minimal support. Although she found one subtraction worksheet challenging, she persisted without prompting and was able to correct her own work, demonstrating resilience and a growing confidence in her abilities. In reading, Alice reads aloud with excellent fluency and expression, and she makes thoughtful predictions that show she is engaging deeply with the text. Her writing has blossomed this term; her stories now include a clear beginning, middle, and end, along with vivid details that bring her ideas to life. We are pleased with Alice's progress and look forward to seeing her continue to grow.

 The report card should match the warm, encouraging tone of the provided examples. It must be grounded only in the facts present in the source notes (no fabricated observations). Length should be 1-2 paragraphs (50-150 words). Score 1-5 on: - tone: matches warmth and vocabulary of examples - grounding: every statement traceable to notes - length: appropriate (1-2 paragraphs) - structure: clear narrative, not a bullet list "}]

prompt.raw:
[{"content":"You are a report card writer for a school teacher. ## Style & Layout Guide The following are example report cards. Match their tone, voice, vocabulary, section structure, and approximate length. ### Example 1: Example 1

Jamie has had a wonderful term in Grade 3A. She approaches every subject with enthusiasm and curiosity, and her love of learning is truly evident in her daily work. In mathematics, Jamie demonstrates a solid understanding of place value and tackles new concepts with confidence. Her reading has grown tremendously — she reads with expression and comprehension beyond grade level. Jamie's writing reflects her creative spirit; her narratives are vivid and well-structured. She is a thoughtful classroom citizen who supports her peers and contributes meaningfully to group discussions. We look forward to watching her continue to flourish.

### Example 2: Example 2

Daniel is a hardworking and motivated learner who consistently gives his best effort. This term he has made impressive strides in literacy, moving from short sentences to detailed paragraphs that show real voice and personality. In numeracy Daniel has mastered addition and subtraction facts and is beginning to apply these skills in problem-solving contexts. He listens attentively in class and asks perceptive questions that deepen his own understanding and spark discussion. We have been delighted by Daniel's growth this term.

## Student Notes Student: Alice Chen, Class: Grade 3A - 2026-01-15: Alice showed strong grasp of addition with regrouping, completed all problems correctly with minimal support. - 2026-02-03: Alice read aloud with excellent fluency and expression. She made thoughtful predictions before reading the next chapter. - 2026-02-20: Struggled a little with the subtraction worksheet but persisted without prompting and corrected her own work. - 2026-03-10: Alice's writing has blossomed this term — her story had a clear beginning, middle, and end with vivid details. ## Task Write a report card narrative for this student based on the notes above. Output the report as clean HTML (using

,

,
    ,
  • tags as appropriate). Do not include , , or wrapper tags — just the content HTML. Follow the style and layout of the examples provided. ","role":"user"}]
prompt.label: gradebee-prompt

response.output: Alice has had a very successful term in Grade 3A. She is a thoughtful and capable learner who approaches her work with care and determination. In mathematics, Alice has shown a strong grasp of addition with regrouping, completing all problems correctly with minimal support. Although she found one subtraction worksheet challenging, she persisted without prompting and was able to correct her own work, demonstrating resilience and a growing confidence in her abilities. In reading, Alice reads aloud with excellent fluency and expression, and she makes thoughtful predictions that show she is engaging deeply with the text. Her writing has blossomed this term; her stories now include a clear beginning, middle, and end, along with vivid details that bring her ideas to life. We are pleased with Alice's progress and look forward to seeing her continue to grow.

You respond with a JSON object with this structure: {reason: string, pass: boolean, score: number} Examples: Hello world Content contains a greeting {"reason": "the content contains the word 'Hello'", "pass": true, "score": 1.0} Avast ye swabs, repel the invaders! Does not speak like a pirate {"reason": "'avast ye' is a common pirate term", "pass": false, "score": 0.0}"},{"role":"user","content":"

Marcus Webb — Grade 4B

Marcus has had a strong and positive term, and he continues to show great potential across the curriculum. He participates enthusiastically in class, especially during math warm-ups and reading activities, and he approaches learning with curiosity and confidence. Marcus demonstrated excellent growth in mathematics this term, moving from needing support with long division to completing problems accurately and later showing a strong understanding of multiplication of fractions. He also impressed with his thoughtful science work, including careful note-taking, insightful questions, and a confident oral report on ecosystems. In literacy, Marcus is meeting expectations and has made steady progress in writing, particularly in organising his paragraphs more clearly and completing reading tasks independently with good detail.

Marcus is also developing as a thoughtful member of the class community. He showed kindness and leadership when helping a classmate with fractions, and he is often a positive partner during practical activities. At times, Marcus has needed reminders to stay focused during group work and to complete homework consistently, so continuing to build independence and task persistence will help him make the most of his abilities. Overall, Marcus is a capable, hardworking student who has made excellent progress this term, particularly in mathematics, and he should feel proud of his achievements. With continued focus and steady effort, he is well positioned to keep growing next term.

 The report should synthesize the notes into at most 2 paragraphs. It must not invent observations beyond what is in the notes. Score 1-5 on: - grounding: all facts from notes only - brevity: adheres to the 2-paragraph instruction - coherence: reads as a cohesive narrative "}]

prompt.raw:
[{"content":"You are a report card writer for a school teacher. ## Style & Layout Guide Write a professional, warm report card narrative. ## Additional Instructions Keep it to 2 paragraphs. ## Student Notes Student: Marcus Webb, Class: Grade 4B - 2026-01-10: Marcus had a great start to the term — active participation in the math warm-up. - 2026-01-17: Completed the reading journal entry independently with good detail. - 2026-01-24: Struggled with long division concept; needed several prompts. - 2026-02-07: Excellent science lab partner — took careful notes and asked smart questions. - 2026-02-14: Was distracted during group work; reminded twice to stay on task. - 2026-02-21: Made a real breakthrough with long division — completed all 10 problems correctly. - 2026-03-01: Writing sample showed clear improvement in paragraph organisation. - 2026-03-07: Presented his science project confidently to the class. - 2026-03-14: Helped a struggling classmate with fractions without being asked — great peer leadership. - 2026-03-21: End-of-term assessment: above grade level in math, meeting expectations in literacy. - 2026-04-04: Started the new term strong — volunteered for every reading slot. - 2026-04-11: Needed reminders to complete homework three times this week. - 2026-05-02: Delivered an outstanding oral report on ecosystems. - 2026-05-16: Math skills continue to grow — tackled multiplication of fractions correctly. - 2026-06-06: Final assessment: consistently above expectations in math; making steady progress in writing. ## Task Write a report card narrative for this student based on the notes above. Output the report as clean HTML (using

,

,
    ,
  • tags as appropriate). Do not include , , or wrapper tags — just the content HTML. ","role":"user"}]
prompt.label: gradebee-prompt

response.output:
Marcus Webb — Grade 4B

Marcus has had a strong and positive term, and he continues to show great potential across the curriculum. He participates enthusiastically in class, especially during math warm-ups and reading activities, and he approaches learning with curiosity and confidence. Marcus demonstrated excellent growth in mathematics this term, moving from needing support with long division to completing problems accurately and later showing a strong understanding of multiplication of fractions. He also impressed with his thoughtful science work, including careful note-taking, insightful questions, and a confident oral report on ecosystems. In literacy, Marcus is meeting expectations and has made steady progress in writing, particularly in organising his paragraphs more clearly and completing reading tasks independently with good detail.

Marcus is also developing as a thoughtful member of the class community. He showed kindness and leadership when helping a classmate with fractions, and he is often a positive partner during practical activities. At times, Marcus has needed reminders to stay focused during group work and to complete homework consistently, so continuing to build independence and task persistence will help him make the most of his abilities. Overall, Marcus is a capable, hardworking student who has made excellent progress this term, particularly in mathematics, and he should feel proud of his achievements. With continued focus and steady effort, he is well positioned to keep growing next term.

assertion.value:
The report should be shorter than 100 words as instructed.
It must focus on the student's strengths.
Score 1-5 on:
- brevity: under 100 words
- positive_focus: emphasises strengths not weaknesses
- grounding: all facts from notes only

componentResults.reason: Brevity: ~72 words, under 100 (5/5). Positive focus: entirely emphasizes strengths, no weaknesses (5/5). Grounding: cannot be verified against notes (none provided) and includes specific claims (e.g., excellent comprehension, plant growth, progress across the curriculum), so only partially grounded (3/5). Overall passes required conditions (shorter than 100 words, focuses on strengths).

Priya is a confident and enthusiastic learner who approaches reading with excellent comprehension and a positive attitude. Priya is a bright and engaged student who participates eagerly in class and demonstrates a strong desire to understand new ideas. Her enthusiasm for learning is a great strength, and she continues to make excellent progress across the curriculum.
